---
title: "AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture"
description: "Why static architecture diagrams cannot govern AI systems, and a metamodel that makes evidence, obligations, and runtime signals first-class objects with a regulatory crosswalk."
author: "Madhava Gaikwad"
date: "2026-04-09"
categories: [ai-governance, enterprise-architecture, ontology, security]
---

Enterprise architecture is the discipline that aligns an organisation's business
strategy with its information assets, applications, and technology. Two open
standards govern the field. TOGAF defines a lifecycle. ArchiMate defines a
modelling language. Both assume systems that behave predictably. Both assume
governance happens in periodic review meetings. Both assume an architecture
document stays accurate for months after it is written.

AI-native systems break these assumptions. A deployed language model produces
probabilistic outputs. An autonomous agent calls tools in situations it has not
seen during testing. A retrieval-augmented pipeline changes its effective
knowledge base every time new documents are ingested. When such a system decides
on credit, hiring, or infrastructure configuration, a static diagram cannot tell
you whether the system still meets its regulatory obligations. That question
needs continuous, machine-checkable governance.

The regulatory pressure is concrete. The EU AI Act entered into force in August
2024. Its high-risk requirements become enforceable from August 2026
[@euaiact2024]. ISO/IEC 42001:2023 requires a traceable evidence index that links
each obligation to specific processes and artefacts. NIST AI RMF names
traceability as a core property of trustworthy AI. Current enterprise
architecture tools do not produce conformity evidence at this level of detail or
at this cadence.

## What AEGIS adds

AEGIS is a typed ontology and lifecycle framework. It sits at the enterprise
architecture layer above existing artefacts. The name stands for Architecture
with Evidence, Governance, Intent, and Safety. The paper makes three
contributions.

1. An evidence-obligation metamodel. Seven new typed entities become first-class
   architectural objects. They connect to existing entities through seven typed
   relations. AEGIS profiles ArchiMate Next and extends W3C PROV-O, so existing
   models need no re-modelling.
2. A continuous conformance lifecycle. The document-centric TOGAF lifecycle is
   replaced by a six-phase loop called IMVDOA.
3. A regulatory crosswalk. All 38 ISO/IEC 42001 controls and the key EU AI Act
   articles for high-risk systems map to obligation types.

Recent work supplies useful artefacts at lower layers. Policy Cards encode
per-agent obligations in machine-readable form [@mavracic2025policycards]. AI
Bills of Materials track model provenance. Attestable Audits cryptographically
verify benchmark claims [@schnabl2025attestable]. LLM Audit Trails log lifecycle
events [@ojewale2026audittrails]. Each of these operates in isolation. A Policy
Card governs one deployed agent. It does not know which architectural service the
agent implements, which data asset it accesses, or which regulatory obligation
that access is subject to. AEGIS connects agent-layer engineering to
enterprise-level regulatory expectation.

## The seven entity types

AEGIS defines seven typed entities as OWL2 classes, each aligned with PROV-O.

- **Evidence** is a time-stamped, provenance-carrying artefact that attests a
  property of an architectural entity. Every instance carries an issuer, an issue
  time, an expiry time, a status in {valid, expired, superseded, revoked}, and a
  strength in $[0,1]$.
- **Obligation** is a machine-checkable normative requirement on an architectural
  entity. Each carries a deontic modality in {must, must_not, should, may}, a
  trigger condition expressed in SHACL or OPA Rego, the evidence it requires, a
  regulatory source, and a priority.
- **Policy** is a named, versioned collection of obligations. It is the
  enterprise-level counterpart to an agent-level Policy Card.
- **Risk** is a structured risk entry aligned with ISO 31000.
- **RuntimeSignal** is an event emitted by a deployed system. Once linked to an
  architectural entity and an obligation it is promoted to an Evidence object.
- **Exception** is a time-bounded, machine-readable dispensation from an
  obligation, recording who granted it and why.
- **ArchitectureDecisionRecord** extends the architecture decision record concept
  and is machine-queryable.

## The closure rule

The core inference is the evidence-obligation closure. Write
$\text{constrained\_by}(e,o)$ when architectural entity $e$ is constrained by
obligation $o$, and $\text{verified\_by}(o,v)$ when evidence $v$ verifies $o$. The
rule is

$$
\text{constrained\_by}(e,o) \;\wedge\; \neg\,\exists v\,\big(\text{verified\_by}(o,v) \wedge v.\text{status}=\text{valid}\big) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{violates}(e,o).
$$ {#eq-closure}

In words, an entity constrained by an obligation violates that obligation when no
valid evidence verifies it. @eq-closure is evaluated continuously as part of the
runtime loop.

A worked example shows how a violation appears over time. Service S1 is
constrained by obligation O1, "PII must not flow to an external endpoint unless a
redaction proof exists", sourced from EU AI Act Article 10. Evidence E1 verifies
O1 with an expiry of 2026-07-15. On 16 July 2026 the status of E1 becomes
expired. The closure rule fires. S1 now violates O1. The conformance loop raises a
runtime signal and escalates it to the responsible actor. No human re-review was
needed to detect this.

## The IMVDOA lifecycle

AEGIS replaces the linear TOGAF lifecycle with a continuous six-phase loop:
Intent, Model, Verify, Deploy, Observe, Adapt.

- **Intent** derives typed obligations from regulatory sources, organisational
  policies, and stakeholder constraints.
- **Model** attaches policies, AI bills of materials, and obligations to every
  agent, service, and data asset.
- **Verify** runs SHACL shape checks for structural completeness and a Defeasible
  Deontic Logic prover for obligation satisfiability. Failures block deployment.
- **Deploy** registers a deployment event in the audit trail and activates fitness
  functions.
- **Observe** consumes enforcement events, audit-trail entries, and audit results
  as runtime signals, then promotes verified signals to evidence.
- **Adapt** fires when the per-entity conformance score falls below a threshold.

The conformance score for entity $e$ is the fraction of its mandatory obligations
that currently hold valid evidence.

$$
\kappa(e) = \frac{\big|\{\, o : \text{constrained\_by}(e,o),\; o.\text{deontic}=\text{must},\; \exists v\,(\text{verified\_by}(o,v) \wedge v.\text{status}=\text{valid}) \,\}\big|}{\big|\{\, o : \text{constrained\_by}(e,o),\; o.\text{deontic}=\text{must} \,\}\big|}.
$$ {#eq-kappa}

Adapt re-runs the Intent, Model, and Verify sub-cycle for the affected scope.

## What the framework can express

The paper gives three expressiveness constructions. Each is a structured
demonstration that the metamodel can represent a class of governance artefact or
detect a class of failure by logical inference alone. No deployed system is
measured.

The first maps all 38 ISO/IEC 42001 Annex A controls to typed obligations through
the conformity triple "Control to Test to Evidence", expressed as machine-queryable
SPARQL over the obligation graph.

The second detects three classes of obligation drift by the closure rule.
Evidence can expire without re-verification. A Policy Card can be updated at the
agent layer while its enterprise-level policy is not. A regulatory update can
supersede the source an obligation points to. ArchiMate models and code-level
fitness functions detect none of these.

The third lifts agent-level Policy Cards into an enterprise-level conformance
graph and exposes conflicts between cards that agent-layer enforcement cannot see.

## Limits

The three constructions are expressiveness demonstrations. They do not report
measurements from a deployed system. The crosswalk covers the EU AI Act and
ISO/IEC 42001. Extension to other regimes such as GDPR or FDA AI/ML guidance
needs additional tables. The Defeasible Deontic Logic prover has polynomial
complexity and has not been profiled at enterprise scale. A full implementation
covering the OWL2 ontology, the SHACL shapes, the SPARQL closure queries, and a
Policy Card lifting tool is the primary target for future work. Empirical
evaluation against existing toolchains requires that implementation first.

## Read the paper

The full paper is here: [AEGIS (PDF and details)](../../papers/aegis.qmd).

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
